text:
CT ABDOMEN AND PELVIS WITH INTRAVENOUS CONTRAST

CLINICAL INDICATION: 53-year-old female with history of FIGO stage IIIC high-grade serous ovarian carcinoma, status post primary debulking surgery achieving complete gross resection, followed by 6 cycles of carboplatin/paclitaxel with complete biochemical and radiographic response. Currently on niraparib maintenance therapy (approximately 6 months). Surveillance imaging to evaluate for disease recurrence. CA-125 14 U/mL (reported as within normal limits).

COMPARISON: CT abdomen/pelvis with contrast performed at completion of chemotherapy (approximately 6 months prior); PET-CT at end of treatment (approximately 6 months prior).

TECHNIQUE: Multidetector CT of the abdomen and pelvis was performed following administration of 100 mL of iohexol intravenous contrast material with portal venous phase acquisition. Coronal and sagittal multiplanar reformations were generated. Oral contrast was not administered.

FINDINGS:

LUNGS AND LOWER THORAX: The lung bases are clear bilaterally. No pleural effusions. No pleural nodularity. No pulmonary nodules are seen at the lung bases. The visualized lower mediastinum is unremarkable.

LIVER AND BILIARY SYSTEM: The liver is normal in size and demonstrates homogeneous enhancement. No focal hepatic lesions identified. No capsular nodularity or surface irregularity to suggest peritoneal implants on the liver surface. The hepatic parenchyma appears uniform throughout. The gallbladder is present and unremarkable. No biliary ductal dilation. No perihepatic fluid collection.

SPLEEN: Normal in size and attenuation. No focal splenic lesions.

PANCREAS: Unremarkable throughout its length. No pancreatic ductal dilation or peripancreatic inflammatory changes.

KIDNEYS AND ADRENALS: Both kidneys enhance symmetrically and show normal corticomedullary differentiation. No hydronephrosis. No renal masses. Bilateral adrenal glands are normal in morphology without discrete nodularity.

GASTROINTESTINAL TRACT: The stomach and visualized small bowel loops appear normal in caliber without wall thickening or obstruction. No bowel wall thickening or mesenteric haziness to suggest carcinomatous involvement. The large bowel is not distended. No pneumatosis.

PERITONEUM AND MESENTERY: No ascites. The peritoneal surfaces are smooth throughout. No peritoneal nodules, implants, or omental thickening identified. The omental fat appears normal in density and distribution — notably, no residual omental haziness or nodularity is seen in the area of prior omentectomy. The paracolic gutters are clear bilaterally. The subdiaphragmatic spaces are clear. The diaphragmatic surfaces appear smooth without nodular thickening; no discrete implants are identified along the right or left hemidiaphragm.

LYMPH NODES: No enlarged lymph nodes in the retroperitoneal, para-aortic, or pelvic chains. A few small mesenteric lymph nodes measuring up to 6 mm in short axis are within normal limits and appear similar to prior. No pelvic sidewall lymphadenopathy.

PELVIS: Post-surgical changes are noted, consistent with the patient's history of total abdominal hysterectomy and bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy. The vaginal cuff is intact without thickening or adjacent soft tissue mass. No adnexal masses (consistent with prior bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy). The bladder is decompressed but demonstrates no focal wall abnormality or intraluminal filling defect where assessable. No pelvic free fluid. No suspicious pelvic soft tissue masses.

VASCULATURE: The aorta and iliac vessels are patent. Mild aortic atherosclerosis without aneurysmal dilation.

BONE AND SOFT TISSUES: No suspicious osseous lesions. The visible soft tissues of the abdominal wall are unremarkable. Subcutaneous tissues without edema or mass.

IMPRESSION:

1. No evidence of peritoneal carcinomatosis, ascites, or lymphadenopathy. The peritoneal and diaphragmatic surfaces, omental bed, and paracolic gutters are all clear — no recurrent or residual disease identified.

2. No hepatic surface implants or parenchymal liver lesions. No pleural effusion or pulmonary nodules at the lung bases.

3. Stable post-surgical changes in the pelvis, without concerning soft tissue in or around the vaginal cuff.

4. Overall appearances are consistent with continued complete response, stable and unchanged from the end-of-treatment imaging obtained approximately 6 months ago.
